primary_diagnosis.name: Severe seborrhoeic dermatitis with anterior blepharitis

pathophysiology_summary: Seborrhoeic dermatitis is a chronic relapsing inflammatory response to commensal Malassezia yeast in sebaceous-rich sites, producing poorly-marginated erythematous plaques with greasy yellow scale on the scalp, nasolabial folds, glabella/eyebrows and central chest, flaring with stress and cold/dry weather. Here it also involves the eyelid margins as anterior blepharitis (greasy crusts on the lash bases, lids sticking together, mild conjunctival injection). Discriminators: greasy yellow scale with nasolabial-fold/eyelid involvement distinguishes it from psoriasis (thick dry silvery scale, Auspitz sign); the greasy scale and nasolabial-fold involvement distinguish it from rosacea (which spares the nasolabial folds and lacks scale); and the absence of monomorphic punched-out erosions/intact vesicles and fever argues against eczema herpeticum. Must-not-miss: periorbital/orbital cellulitis (hot hard spreading swelling, fever, painful/limited eye movements) and herpetic keratitis (severe eye pain, photophobia, blurred vision) — a telehealth provider cannot perform a slit-lamp exam, so an ocular safety-net is essential; here only mild lid crusting with normal vision is present. Management is an antifungal (ketoconazole shampoo to scalp/face), a short course of a LOW-potency facial corticosteroid (limit duration to avoid facial skin atrophy), and lid hygiene (warm compresses/gentle lid cleaning), with reassurance about the chronic relapsing but controllable nature.

differential.evidence_basis: Greasy yellow scale in a seborrhoeic distribution with anterior blepharitis, stress/winter trigger.
Screen for severe eye pain/photophobia/blurred vision — cannot slit-lamp remotely.
Screen for hot hard spreading facial swelling/fever or punched-out erosions.
Thick dry silvery scale, not greasy yellow scale in nasolabial folds/eyelids.
Spares the nasolabial folds and lacks greasy scale.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Diagnose seborrhoeic dermatitis with blepharitis, screen the eye (keratitis) and for periorbital cellulitis/eczema herpeticum, and treat with antifungal + short low-potency facial steroid + lid hygiene.

differential.evidence_basis: Greasy scale + lid crusting; only mild eye redness with normal vision; no cellulitis/herpetic features.
Currently no keratitis features but cannot be fully excluded remotely — remains a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: ketoconazole + short low-potency facial steroid + lid hygiene; ocular/periorbital safety-net.

red_flags.description: Serious eye involvement / herpetic keratitis (severe eye pain, photophobia, blurred vision)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cannot be excluded remotely (no slit-lamp); vision-threatening if present.

red_flags.description: Periorbital/orbital cellulitis or eczema herpeticum (hot hard spreading swelling/fever; clustered punched-out erosions)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Both are emergencies needing urgent treatment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reassuring the eye is fine without screening for keratitis (no slit-lamp remotely).
Prescribing potent/long-course facial steroids causing atrophy/perioral dermatitis.
Mislabelling greasy-scaled seborrhoeic dermatitis as psoriasis or rosacea.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prescribe ketoconazole shampoo (scalp/face) + a short low-potency facial corticosteroid + lid hygiene, and reassure; urgent eye/ED care if severe eye pain/photophobia/blurred vision or periorbital cellulitis/eczema-herpeticum features develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing keratitis/periorbital cellulitis, or overusing facial steroids.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Seborrhoeic dermatitis: topical antifungals (ketoconazole) ± short low-potency topical corticosteroid for the face; treat blepharitis with lid hygiene; it is chronic and relapsing.
